title: Ending Drug Trafficking in Our Communities Act
short_title: Ending Drug Trafficking in Our Communities Act
plain_summary: This bill gives money to a program that helps fight drug trafficking in areas where it is a big problem. The program will get 400 million dollars every year until 2031. The person in charge of the program must find the best ways to stop drug trafficking and share these ideas with all the areas in the program. This will help police and communities work better together to catch drug dealers and prevent drug crimes.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committees on the Judiciary, and Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Ending Drug Trafficking in Our Communities Act.

sections.title: Section 2 High Intensity Drug Trafficking Areas Program
sections.text: This section makes changes to an existing program that helps fight drug trafficking in areas where it is a big problem. These areas are called High Intensity Drug Trafficking Areas or HIDTAs. First, this section provides money for the program. It gives four hundred million dollars each year from fiscal year two thousand twenty six through two thousand thirty one. Second, this section requires the Director of the program to find and share good practices that work well. Starting in fiscal year two thousand twenty six, the Director must do several things each year. The Director must look at each good practice to see if it could make HIDTA work better and more efficiently. This includes practices for finding and investigating people who sell illegal drugs that cause overdoses in HIDTA areas. It also includes practices for sharing information and working together when federal, state, territorial, tribal, and local agencies respond to crimes involving illegal drugs and guns used in crimes. The Director must also look at practices for starting and checking programs that prevent substance use disorders based on proven research. For each good practice that could help, the Director must develop it so all HIDTA areas can use it. Then the Director must share each developed practice with all HIDTA areas.
